annotations.label: Fourier analysis

anchor.snippet: is the study of the way general functions

annotations.note: Fourier analysis as a field/area of study is a meta-description, not a single formalizable statement, though Mathlib has extensive Fourier machinery.

annotations.label: Fourier synthesis

anchor.snippet: the operation of rebuilding the function from these pieces

annotations.note: Rebuilding f from its transform is exactly the inverse transform whose correctness is the Fourier inversion theorem.

annotations.label: Fourier transformation

anchor.snippet: The decomposition process itself is called a Fourier transformation

annotations.note: The Fourier transform is defined as fourierIntegral via an additive character and bilinear pairing.

annotations.label: Transforms are linear and unitary (Parseval/Plancherel)

anchor.snippet: The transforms are linear operators and, with proper normalization, are unitary as well

annotations.note: The L² Fourier transform is built as a linear isometry equivalence (unitary), with Plancherel as norm_fourier_eq/inner_fourier_eq.

annotations.label: Exponentials are eigenfunctions of differentiation

anchor.snippet: The exponential functions are eigenfunctions of differentiation

annotations.note: hasDerivAt_exp states the derivative of exp equals exp, i.e. exp is an eigenfunction of differentiation with eigenvalue 1 (cf. Real.hasDerivAt_fourierChar for the 2πiξ eigenvalue).

annotations.label: Convolution theorem

anchor.snippet: By the convolution theorem , Fourier transforms turn the complicated convolution operation into simple multiplication

annotations.note: The Fourier transform of a convolution equals the pointwise product of transforms.

annotations.label: JPEG compression via discrete cosine transform

anchor.snippet: JPEG compression uses a variant of the Fourier transformation

annotations.note: Mathlib has no discrete cosine transform or JPEG-related formalization.

annotations.label: Five variants of Fourier analysis

anchor.snippet: There are five different variants of Fourier analysis depending on the characteristics of the input signal

annotations.note: This taxonomy of transform variants is expository, not a formal mathematical statement.

annotations.label: DFT is the only numerically computable variant

anchor.snippet: The DFT is the only variant that can be calculated numerically

annotations.note: This is an informal computability remark, not a formalized theorem.

annotations.label: Continuous-time Fourier transform formula

anchor.snippet: is given by the complex number

annotations.note: Real.fourierIntegral is the integral defining the continuous Fourier transform on ℝ.

annotations.label: Inverse Fourier transform formula

anchor.snippet: which is the inverse transform formula

annotations.note: Real.fourierIntegralInv is the integral defining the inverse Fourier transform.

annotations.label: Fourier transform of a periodic function is a Dirac comb

anchor.snippet: The Fourier transform of a periodic function

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Dirac comb or distributional Fourier transform of a periodic function (Poisson summation is the closest scalar identity).

annotations.label: Fourier series

anchor.snippet: The inverse transform, known as Fourier series , is a representation of

annotations.note: fourierCoeff and the series ∑ fourierCoeff n • fourier n give the Fourier series representation, shown to sum to f when coefficients are summable.

annotations.label: Sufficient condition for recovery (dual of Nyquist–Shannon)

anchor.snippet: A sufficient condition for recovering

annotations.note: Mathlib gives summability of Fourier coefficients as a sufficient condition for recovery, but not the bandlimited dual-of-Nyquist sampling condition.

annotations.label: DTFT as dual of the Fourier series

anchor.snippet: The DTFT is the mathematical dual of the time-domain Fourier series

annotations.note: Mathlib has no discrete-time Fourier transform of a sequence into a periodic spectrum.

annotations.label: Poisson summation formula

anchor.snippet: this Fourier series can now be recognized as a form of the Poisson summation formula

annotations.note: Poisson summation is formalized for functions on ℝ with suitable decay (and for Schwartz functions).

annotations.label: Whittaker–Shannon interpolation / perfect recovery

anchor.snippet: A sufficient condition for perfect recovery is that the non-zero portion of

annotations.note: Mathlib has no sampling theorem or Whittaker–Shannon interpolation formula.

annotations.label: DTFT of a periodic sequence is a Dirac comb

anchor.snippet: the DTFT of a periodic sequence

annotations.note: Neither the DTFT nor the Dirac comb is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Discrete Fourier transform of one cycle

anchor.snippet: is customarily known as the DFT of one cycle of

annotations.note: ZMod.dft is the discrete Fourier transform on ℤ/Nℤ as a linear equivalence.

annotations.label: Inverse DFT (discrete Fourier series)

anchor.snippet: The inverse transform, also known as a discrete Fourier series , is given by

annotations.note: The inverse DFT is the symm of ZMod.dft, with formula given by invDFT_def.

annotations.label: DFT computed via FFT

anchor.snippet: The DFT can be computed using a fast Fourier transform (FFT) algorithm

annotations.note: Mathlib has no fast Fourier transform algorithm or its complexity analysis.

annotations.label: Periodic functions have discrete spectra

anchor.snippet: both the Fourier transform and the DTFT comprise only a discrete set of frequency components

annotations.note: Discreteness is implicit in representing periodic functions by ℤ-indexed Fourier coefficients, but there is no theorem stating periodicity implies a discrete spectrum.

annotations.label: One-to-one mapping of even/odd components

anchor.snippet: there is a one-to-one mapping between the four components of a complex time function

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize the even/odd real/imaginary component correspondence under the Fourier transform.

annotations.label: Transform of a real-valued function is conjugate symmetric

anchor.snippet: The transform of a real-valued function

annotations.note: There is no lemma that the Fourier transform of a real-valued function is Hermitian/conjugate-symmetric (only AddCircle.fourier_neg for the basis characters).

annotations.label: Uncertainty principle time–frequency trade-off

anchor.snippet: by the uncertainty principle , there is a trade-off between these

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Fourier/Heisenberg uncertainty principle.

annotations.label: Fourier transform on locally compact abelian groups

anchor.section: Fourier transforms on arbitrary locally compact abelian topological groups
anchor.snippet: the Fourier transform takes functions on a group to functions on the dual group

annotations.note: The dual-group Fourier transform is formalized for finite abelian groups (via characters/PontryaginDuality and ZMod.dft) but not for general locally compact abelian groups.

annotations.label: General convolution theorem

anchor.section: Fourier transforms on arbitrary locally compact abelian topological groups
anchor.snippet: This treatment also allows a general formulation of the convolution theorem

annotations.note: The convolution theorem is formalized on ℝ/vector spaces, but not in the general locally-compact-abelian-group formulation.

annotations.label: Lagrange resolvents as the DFT of order 3

anchor.snippet: which is the DFT of order 3

annotations.note: Although ZMod.dft instantiates a DFT, the identification of Lagrange resolvents with the order-3 DFT is not formalized.
